3 min read
Just now

--

Once you have automated the frontend, the focus shifts to the true heart of your system: the backend. A common mistake is choosing a backend language based on the familiarity of your team rather than the architectural requirements of your product.
Code is for Humans, Not Machines
Never forget that programming languages are, first and foremost, a medium for human communication. Code is a persistent conversation between the developer who wrote it, the colleague who will maintain it, and the architect who must understand the business logic hidden within the implementation. If your language choice makes this communication difficult, your project is already in debt.
The Great Paradigm War: OOP vs. Functional
To design a robust system, you must navigate the competition between two dominant paradigms:

• Object-Oriented Programming (OOP): Designed for heavy, monolithic systems where “entities” (like users, orders, or inventory items) are the building blocks. OOP is fantastic for modeling complex state, but it can quickly become unmanageable if the state becomes too fragmented.

• Functional Programming (FP): With roots in the late 1950s — specifically Lisp, pioneered by the brilliant scientist John McCarthy — FP operates on the principle of immutability. Based on the Church-Turing thesis, FP posits that any system can be built purely from functions that transform input to output without modifying external state.
While pure Lisp offers powerful immutability, it is often criticized for being unreadable and difficult to map to standard enterprise business entities. However, the rigidity of OOP without functional safety is equally dangerous.
The Ideal: The best programming language for modern backends is one that is fundamentally OOP (to handle your entities and polymorphism) but offers first-class functional capabilities (to manage internal state and side effects).
A Brief History of Backend Evolution
The C Legacy: In the 1970s, Dennis Ritchie created C, which became the bedrock of systems programming. Bill Joy, who wrote the Unix network stack and later co-founded Sun Microsystems, was a master of this era.
The Rise of OOP: As complexity grew, C++ introduced OOP to C, but it became unwieldy. In response, Sun Microsystems created Java — a simplified, more structured C++. Microsoft followed with C#, which significantly improved developer productivity by introducing LINQ (Language Integrated Query), effectively bringing SQL-style operations to code collections.
The Dynamic Deviation: Languages like PHP and later Python gained traction because they were easy for novices. They denounced strict polymorphic types in favor of speed and flexibility, but this often made large-scale design prone to runtime errors.
The JavaScript Anomaly: JavaScript was originally designed as a single-threaded language for browsers. When Google pushed it to the server side (via the V8 engine and libuv for I/O), we inherited a language that wasn’t designed for backend concurrency. Concepts like promises were essentially patches to force a single-threaded, blocking language to handle server-side tasks.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Modern Standard: Scala and Java 8+
The industry has realized that using Node.js for backend servers is often a choice made out of convenience rather than performance. To bridge the gap, Martin Odersky designed Scala.
Scala is the ultimate “syncretic” language: it runs on the Java Virtual Machine (JVM), provides the structured power of Java, but incorporates functional concepts — many inspired by Haskell — to ensure code safety and immutability. Similarly, modern Java (8+) and C# have evolved to include significant functional features.
If you are building for the long term, your backend foundation should be Scala, modern Java, or their functional counterparts like Clojure or F#. These languages are designed to handle the scale of modern infrastructure while keeping your code readable and, most importantly, maintainable.
